currentSnapshot.definition: Pleasant, agreeable, or satisfactory; kind and considerate.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Now the most generic of positive adjectives—so broad it is often considered weak. 'Have a nice day' is almost a placeholder.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: She was incredibly nice about the whole misunderstanding.

historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Borrowed from Old French 'nice' (foolish), itself from Latin 'nescius' (ignorant). In early Middle English, calling someone 'nice' was an insult.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: He was a nice fool who knew nothing of the world.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Fastidious, fussy, precise; requiring careful discrimination.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The meaning ameliorated from 'foolish' to 'excessively precise'—someone too particular about details. 'A nice distinction' still echoes this sense.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: She was nice in her choice of words, never permitting an imprecision.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Precise, exact, requiring careful discrimination; subtle. Also: refined, cultured, respectable.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Both meanings coexisted: 'nice' could mean 'exact' (a nice point of law) or 'refined' (a nice young lady).
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: That is a nice distinction which requires careful analysis.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Agreeable, pleasant, charming. The ameliorated sense now dominant.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: Jane Austen famously noted that 'nice' was used too freely. By the 1830s the 'pleasant/agreeable' sense had become dominant, though the 'precise' sense survived in educated registers.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: What a nice evening we had at the Bennets'.

summaryOfChange.longSummary: Nice has undergone one of English's most dramatic ameliorations. It entered Middle English from Old French meaning 'foolish' or 'ignorant'. Over the Early Modern period it shifted to mean 'excessively precise or fussy'. By the 18th century it had taken on the senses of 'refined' and 'discriminating'. By the Regency era the shift to simply 'pleasant' was nearly complete. Today it is the blandest of positive adjectives, almost devoid of specific meaning—a victim of its own success.

keyDates.label: First English recorded use
keyDates.significance: Earliest attested use in English: 'foolish, wanton'. From Latin 'nescius' via Old French 'nice'.

keyDates.label: Johnson's Dictionary
keyDates.significance: Johnson defined 'nice' with multiple senses including 'accurate, scrupulous' — evidence of the fastidious stage.

keyDates.label: Austen's Northanger Abbey written
keyDates.significance: Austen has Henry Tilney lecture Catherine on the 'overuse' of nice: 'a word of almost every meaning' — evidence that the semantic bleaching was already underway.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – nice, adj.
sources.excerpt: Originally: foolish, stupid; lascivious. Later: fastidious, particular. Then: precise, accurate. Finally: pleasant, agreeable.
sources.relevanceNote: Comprehensive etymology tracing the full arc from Latin through Old French to modern English.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – nice, adj. (modern senses)
sources.excerpt: Pleasant, agreeable, satisfactory; kind and considerate. In weakened use: good, attractive.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the completed amelioration and subsequent semantic bleaching.

sources.title: Northanger Abbey
sources.author: Jane Austen
sources.excerpt: "But now really, do not you think Udolpho the nicest book in the world?" ... "The nicest — by which I suppose you mean the neatest. That is not a word I am very partial to."
sources.relevanceNote: Austen's characters debate the meaning of 'nice' — direct literary evidence of semantic change in progress.

sources.title: A Dictionary of the English Language – nice
sources.author: Samuel Johnson
sources.excerpt: NICE. adj. 1. Accurate in judgement to minute exactness; precise to affectation. 2. Squeamish; over-careful in choice. 3. Delicate; requiring scrupulous exactness.
sources.relevanceNote: Johnson's entry captures the 'fastidious/precise' stage before the full amelioration.

sources.title: Middle English Dictionary – nice
sources.excerpt: nice: foolish, silly, ignorant; lascivious; wanton.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the earliest English sense: the insult 'foolish/ignorant' before amelioration began.

relatedWorks.title: Northanger Abbey
relatedWorks.creator: Jane Austen
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Contains the famous dialogue where Henry Tilney critiques the overuse of 'nice', providing a literary snapshot of semantic change in progress.

relatedPages.title: Amelioration
relatedPages.summary: How and why word meanings become more positive over time.

relatedPages.title: Semantic Drift
relatedPages.summary: The broad phenomenon of word meanings shifting across time.

relatedConcepts.label: awful
relatedConcepts.note: Awful underwent the opposite journey: from reverential positive to negative.

relatedConcepts.label: pretty
relatedConcepts.note: Pretty also bleached from specific (cunning/clever) to generic positive.

ambiguityNotes: The precise/discriminating sense ('a nice distinction') survives in formal and academic writing today alongside the dominant 'pleasant' sense.
In some British dialects, 'nice' can still carry the nuance of 'fastidious' or 'fussy'.

timelineEvents.title: Enters English as an insult
timelineEvents.summary: Borrowed from Old French 'nice' (foolish, ignorant), itself from Latin 'nescius'. In early use, calling someone nice was to call them a fool.

timelineEvents.title: The fastidious turn
timelineEvents.summary: The meaning ameliorated from 'foolish' to 'excessively particular'—someone overly precise about detail. The word began its long upward journey.

timelineEvents.title: Johnson captures the precise sense
timelineEvents.summary: Johnson's Dictionary gives 'accurate in judgment to minute exactness' as the primary sense, showing the word at its most refined before popularisation.

timelineEvents.title: Austen's complaint
timelineEvents.summary: In Northanger Abbey, Austen lampoons the word's overuse, showing that 'pleasant/agreeable' was already dominant and that the older precise sense was slipping.
